title: The Jolly Dead March
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I ever be worthy or famous,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which Iן¿½m sadly beginning to doubt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the angel whose place ן¿½tis to name us
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall say to my spirit, ן¿½Pass out!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wish for no snivן¿½lling about me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (My work was the work of the land),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I hope that my country will shout me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The price of a decent brass band.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thump! thump! of the drum and ן¿½Ta-ra-rit,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thump! thump! and the music, itן¿½s grand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If only in dreams, or in spirit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To ride or march after the band!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And myself and my mourners go straying,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And strolling and drifting along
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a band in the front of us playing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The tune of an old battle song!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I ask for no ן¿½turn-outן¿½ to bear me;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I ask not for railings or slabs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And spare me! my country, oh, spare me!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hearse and the long string of cabs!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I ask not the baton or ן¿½startsן¿½ of
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The bore with the musical ear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the music thatן¿½s blown from the hearts of
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The men who work hard and drink beer.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And let ן¿½em strike up ן¿½Annie Laurie,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And let them burst out with ן¿½Lang Syneן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Twin voices of sadness and glory,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That have ever been likings of mine.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And give the French war-hymn deep-throated
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Watch of the Germans between,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And let the last mile be devoted
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To ן¿½Britanniaן¿½ and ן¿½Wearing the Green.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And if, in the end, moreן¿½s the pity,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is fame more than money to spare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s a van-man I know in the city
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whoן¿½ll convey me, right side up with care.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ True sons of Australia, and noble,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have gone from the long dusty way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the sole mourner fought down his trouble
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With his pipe on the shaft of the dray.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But let them strike up ן¿½Annie Laurie,ן¿½ &c.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And my spirit will join the procession,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will pause, if it may, on the brink,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor feel the least shade of depression
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the mourners drop out for a drink;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It may be a hot day in December,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or a cold day in June it may be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the drink will but help them remember
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The good points the world missed in me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And help ן¿½em to love ן¿½Annie Laurie,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And help ן¿½em to raise ן¿½Auld Lang Syne,ן¿½ &c.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Unhook the West Portן¿½ for an orphan,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An old digger chorus revive,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If you donן¿½t hear a whoop from the coffin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am not being buried alive.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Iן¿½ll go with a spirit less bitter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than mine own on the earth may have been,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, perhaps, to save trouble, Saint Peter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will pass me, two comrades between.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And let them strike up ן¿½Annie Laurie,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And let ן¿½em burst out with ן¿½Lang Syne,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Twin voices of sadness and glory
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That have ever been likings of mine.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let them swell the French war-hymn deep-throated
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And Iן¿½ll not buck at ן¿½God Save the Queenן¿½),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But let the last mile be devoted
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To ן¿½Britanniaן¿½ and ן¿½Wearing the Green.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thump! thump! of the drums we inherit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ War-drums of my dreams! Oh itן¿½s grand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If only in fancy or spirit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To ride or march after a band!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we, the World-Battlers, go straying
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And loving and laughing along,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With Hope in the lead of us playing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The tune of a life-battle song!
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
